title: Willow hurdle garden panel
subtitle: A woven willow panel for temporary fencing, windbreaks, and garden screening
excerpt: A willow hurdle is a woven panel built between vertical stakes fixed into the ground, using long willow rods woven in a continuous wave from one edge to the other. Traditional hurdle dimensions are 180 cm wide by 90 cm high; a garden screening panel can be made at any scale. A faster project than a basket since no complex bordering or handle work is needed.
sourceNotes: Wattle hurdle construction documented in British rural craft traditions from the medieval period; Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885); hurdles used for sheep folding documented in agricultural records.

glossaryTerms.definition: Single-rod weaving in which one rod passes over and under each stake alternately. On a hurdle, the rod continues to the opposite edge and returns, producing a continuous wave of weaving.
A three-rod weave producing a strong, ribbed band. Used at the top and bottom of a hurdle to lock the weaving in place and prevent the rods slipping off the stakes.

Long, flexible willow rods used for weaving hurdles and large baskets. Lengths of 2 to 3 metres are standard for hurdle work; they are long enough to run from edge to edge of the hurdle without joining.
A pointed hazel rod driven vertically into the ground or a horizontal rail to form the structural upright around which withies are woven. Stakes are the fixed element; withies are the weavers.
A blunt-tipped pointed tool used to open the weave to tuck rod ends into the structure. On a hurdle, used to secure the end of each rod behind a stake.

projectSchedule.title: Set stakes and weave the hurdle
projectSchedule.body: Drive stakes into the ground or a template, weave from bottom to top with withies, rap each row down. Three to four hours for a 90 x 90 cm panel.

content.text: A willow hurdle is built around a row of fixed

wave from edge to edge. Unlike a basket, there is no border to close and no base to weave: the hurdle is open at the top and the weave is locked at the sides by the stake positions. The work is fast once the stakes are in place.

For a 90 cm wide by 90 cm high panel: 9 hazel or ash stakes, 130 cm long (90 cm above the frame plus 40 cm for driving or fixing), pointed at one end. Long willow withies, 2 to 3 metres in length, for the weaving. A sufficient quantity of withies depends on rod thickness; allow 20 to 30 rods for a single-panel height of 90 cm, more if the rods are thin.

A bodkin for tucking rod ends behind stakes. Secateurs for cutting rods. A rapping iron for tamping weavers down after each rod. A beetle mallet for driving stakes into the ground or a base rail, if working on-site.

Drive 9 stakes into a firm base (or into a horizontal rail on a portable hurdle-making frame), evenly spaced at 10 cm intervals across 90 cm. The stakes must be vertical and firm; any lean will introduce a twist into the finished panel. For a garden panel, drive stakes 30 to 40 cm into the ground; the finished hurdle can be cut off at the ground level after use.
of two rounds close to the base to lock the bottom of the weave. The wale produces a firm lower edge that keeps the subsequent randing in position as the panel grows.
Step 3: Rand from edge to edge

Take a long withy and hook its butt end behind the edge stake. Weave it in and out across all 9 stakes to the opposite edge, hook it around that stake, and weave back. The rod travels back and forth across the panel, producing an alternating over-under pattern. When the rod becomes too thin to weave, introduce a new rod alongside it and continue; the old tip lies behind a stake and is cut short.
to maintain an even wave. Tamp each rod down with the rapping iron before starting the next. The hurdle panel builds row by row up the stakes.
Step 4: Finish with a top wale
When the panel reaches the target height (90 cm), work a three-rod wale of two rounds to lock the top of the weave. Tuck the ends of the

The stake tops that protrude above the top wale can be cut level with the wale, left at full height (for a rustic appearance and to allow the hurdle to be driven further into the ground if needed), or bent and tucked back into the weave to produce a neat finished top edge.

No finish is needed. A willow hurdle used outdoors will weather to silver-grey over its first season. Hazel stakes will last four to six years in the ground; willow weaving lasts longer if kept from prolonged soil contact.
Make the panel 180 cm wide for a full hurdle suitable as a windbreak panel; use 17 stakes at the same 10 cm spacing.
Add a decorative band of three-rod waling at the centre of the hurdle for structural reinforcement and visual interest.
Use mixed willow colours (buff and brown) in alternating rows for a two-tone striped panel.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems when weaving a hurdle panel.

items.symptom: The panel bows outward in the middle as the sides weave tighter
items.cause: The edge rods are wrapping around the end stakes more tightly than the rods weaving across the body, pulling the outer stakes inward.
items.fix: Check stake spacing every 20 cm of height and correct any closing-in by gently pushing stakes back to the correct spacing. End stakes can be anchored by tying them to a horizontal rail during weaving.

items.symptom: The weave is dense and tight at the bottom but loose at the top
items.cause: Rapping was not applied consistently, or rods were not selected for consistent thickness through the height of the panel.
items.fix: Use the rapping iron more firmly on each row. Select thicker rods for the top of the panel to compensate for reduced pack density at height.

items.symptom: Withies run out before reaching the opposite edge
items.cause: The rods are thinner than expected, or longer rods are needed.
items.fix: Introduce a new rod alongside the existing one, hook it around the last stake the old rod reached, and continue from there. Allow the old rod tip to lie behind the stake and cut short.
